abstract: This article addresses a persistent challenge in tertiary English language education: the noticeable lack of qualitative progress in the written and oral performance of certain students. Research findings presented in this paper confirm a trend in which most learners reach a steady plateau—typically at the B2 level—upon entering university. In response, the authors examine the limitations of prevailing pedagogical methods and advocate for a paradigm shift toward approaches that foster self-confidence, creativity, and linguistic experimentation. The study explores whether such methods can effectively empower students to take greater responsibility for their own learning processes. Evaluation of these instructional changes, reflected in student feedback from the survey, suggests a positive impact on learner engagement and developmental growth.
